about.role: Founder & Managing Director, Altura Management Consulting
about.bio: Jean Bosco Nkusi is the Founder and Managing Director of Altura Management Consulting. A former CEO of MTN Mobile Money Congo, Jean Bosco brings 15+ years of operator-side leadership across African telecom and fintech — with senior roles at MTN and Tigo spanning mobile money, sales and distribution, fintech operations, and pan-African strategy across the WECA region.
about.bio2: As CEO of MTN Mobile Money Congo, he grew the platform to 2.6 million users and over €40 million in annual revenue, and secured the first PSP license in the CEMAC region — a pioneering regulatory milestone for mobile financial services in Central Africa. He was awarded the Knight of the Congolese Order of Merit for his contribution to financial inclusion.
about.bio3: He founded Altura to bring this operator-side experience to a wider set of clients — telecom operators, fintech scale-ups, and B2B SaaS companies navigating African markets — through commercial leadership, strategic advisory, and board mandates.
about.bio4: Current engagements include leading Business Development for FraudBuster's Mobile Money Security business across Africa. Jean Bosco holds an Executive MBA and is based in Brussels. He works in English, French and Kinyarwanda.
about.awardTitle: Knight of the Congolese Order of Merit
about.awardDesc: Awarded for exceptional contribution to financial inclusion and mobile financial services development in the DRC.
about.currentEngagement: Leading Business Development for FraudBuster's Mobile Money Security business across Africa.
about.timeline1Desc: Grew platform to 2.6M users, secured first CEMAC PSP license, drove €40M+ annual revenue.
about.timeline2Desc: Pan-African strategy, fintech operations, and market expansion across West and Central Africa.
about.timeline3Desc: Sales, distribution, and mobile money leadership across multiple African markets.
about.engagement1Role: Business Development Lead — Mobile Money Security, Africa

articles.title: How the First PSP License in CEMAC Changed Mobile Money Forever
articles.excerpt: Securing the first PSP license in the CEMAC region was more than a regulatory achievement — it was a blueprint for how mobile money operators can unlock institutional partnerships, cross-border payments, and long-term regulatory confidence.
articles.lead: In 2021, MTN Mobile Money Congo became the first mobile money operator in the CEMAC region to secure a Payment Service Provider license — a milestone that fundamentally changed the regulatory landscape for digital financial services in Central Africa.
articles.body:
The journey to securing this license was not a straightforward regulatory process. It required years of relationship-building with the BEAC, deep engagement with the DRC's central bank, and a commercial track record that demonstrated the scale and reliability of the mobile money operation.

What made this milestone significant was not just the license itself, but what it unlocked: the ability to operate as a formal financial institution, to partner with banks under a regulated framework, and to position MTN Mobile Money as a credible player in cross-border payment corridors.

For operators still navigating similar processes across the continent, the key lessons are clear: start regulatory engagement early, invest in compliance infrastructure before you need it, and treat the license application as a commercial strategy, not a compliance checkbox.

The CEMAC precedent has since influenced regulatory conversations in East Africa and the ECOWAS region — proof that pioneering regulatory work in one market can reshape the entire continent's approach to mobile financial services.

articles.title: Why Telcos Are Losing the Fintech Race — And How to Fight Back
articles.excerpt: Despite launching mobile money years before dedicated fintechs, many telecom operators are watching their financial services market share erode. The problem isn't technology — it's organizational design.
articles.lead: Mobile operators were the original architects of mobile financial services in Africa. They built the infrastructure, developed the agent networks, and established the trust. So why are dedicated fintechs now outcompeting them in product innovation and customer experience?
articles.body:
The answer lies not in technology gaps but in organizational structure. Most MNOs treat their mobile money units as subsidiaries or business lines — constrained by telco governance, telco procurement cycles, and telco KPIs. Fintechs operate without these constraints.

The operators that are winning — M-Pesa under Safaricom, Wave in Senegal — have either fully separated their financial services entities or given them the autonomy to operate as independent businesses.

For operators still fighting this battle, three things matter most: autonomous P&L structures for the financial services unit, fintech-grade product talent, and a regulatory strategy that pursues full banking or payment licenses.

articles.title: The Real Cost of Mobile Money Fraud — Beyond the Transaction Loss
articles.excerpt: When mobile money operators calculate the cost of fraud, they typically count the direct transaction losses. The real cost — in customer trust, agent network attrition, and regulatory exposure — is three to five times larger.
articles.lead: Mobile money fraud is routinely underestimated because operators measure it in transaction losses. The true cost — when you account for customer churn, agent compensation disputes, call center volumes, and regulatory scrutiny — is dramatically higher.
articles.body:
A major mobile money operator in West Africa found that for every dollar lost to SIM swap fraud, they spent an additional $2.80 in remediation costs. When factoring in customer lifetime value lost to churn, the ratio climbed to 1:4.7.

The strategic implication is clear: fraud prevention is not a cost center, it's a revenue protection strategy. Operators that treat security investment as overhead are making a category error.

The most effective fraud prevention frameworks combine three elements: real-time transaction monitoring with machine learning, agent network scoring systems, and customer education programs that shift first-line defense to users themselves.

articles.title: Five Mistakes B2B SaaS Companies Make Entering African Markets
articles.excerpt: Most European and North American SaaS companies approaching Africa make the same predictable mistakes — and pay for them in 18-month delays, failed partnerships, and wasted commercial investment.
articles.lead: African B2B SaaS is a real and growing market — but it rewards operators who understand its distinct procurement dynamics, decision-making structures, and implementation realities.
articles.body:
Mistake 1: Treating Africa as a single market. Nigeria and Kenya are no more similar than Germany and Turkey.

Mistake 2: Underestimating procurement timelines. Enterprise and government procurement in Africa routinely takes 18–36 months.

Mistake 3: Hiring local salespeople without senior oversight. The commercial leadership gap is real.

Mistake 4: Ignoring connectivity and infrastructure constraints.

Mistake 5: Skipping the regulatory mapping. Every market has specific data residency, payment processing, and technology import regulations.

articles.title: From Operator to Advisor: What Changes, What Doesn't
articles.excerpt: After 15 years running commercial operations for major African telcos, the shift to advisory work revealed something unexpected: the most valuable asset isn't the expertise — it's knowing which expertise matters.
articles.lead: When I founded Altura, I assumed the hardest part of the transition from operator to advisor would be the absence of a team, a budget, and execution authority. It wasn't.
articles.body:
Operator life is about execution. You run teams, manage P&Ls, own KPIs. Advisory is different: your job is to help someone else achieve outcomes through better decisions.

What doesn't change: the fundamental knowledge of how African telco and fintech markets actually work — not as described in analyst reports, but as experienced by the people running the operations.

What does change: the need to be explicit about what you know versus what you think you know. In advisory, you are only as good as the accuracy of your analysis.

articles.title: Navigating the Regulatory Landscape Across 15 African Markets
articles.excerpt: A comparative analysis of mobile financial services regulation across 15 African markets — identifying the four key variables that determine regulatory complexity and timeline.
articles.lead: Regulatory approval for mobile financial services in Africa is not a single process — it is 15 different processes, each with distinct requirements, timelines, and political dynamics.
articles.body:
Four variables consistently determine the complexity and timeline of the process:

1. Central bank independence. Markets where the central bank operates with genuine independence from political direction tend to have more predictable regulatory processes.

2. Existing regulatory framework maturity. Markets with established e-money or PSP frameworks — Kenya, Nigeria, Ghana — have clearer paths to approval.

3. Competitive dynamics. In markets with dominant incumbents, regulatory approval processes can be significantly influenced by competitive lobbying.

4. Technical infrastructure at the regulator. Markets with technically capable regulatory teams can engage with complex license applications more quickly.

articles.title: The Due Diligence Gaps That Kill African Telecom M&A Deals
articles.excerpt: Most M&A failures in African telecom and fintech are predictable — and preventable. The three most common due diligence failures that turn promising acquisitions into expensive lessons.
articles.lead: M&A activity in African telecom and fintech has accelerated significantly. So have deal failures — not at the signing stage, but 18 months after close.
articles.body:
Three due diligence failures account for the majority of post-close disappointments:

1. Agent network quality assessment. Active agent rates — agents transacting at least once per month — typically run at 40–60% of the registered base.

2. Regulatory license portability. Operating licenses in African markets are almost never automatically transferable upon a change of ownership.

3. Revenue quality assessment. In markets with high inflation or currency volatility, revenue figures reported in USD or EUR can mask underlying local currency deterioration.
